name: Why is my new floor creaking, and can it be fixed without tearing everything out?
headline: Why is my new floor creaking, and can it be fixed without tearing everything out?

mainEntity.name: Why is my new floor creaking, and can it be fixed without tearing everything out?
mainEntity.text: Why is my new floor creaking, and can it be fixed without tearing everything out?

acceptedAnswer.text: Creaking or squeaking under new floors almost always points to movement in the subfloor or the new floor itself. If the squeaks were there before, the installer may have laid new flooring over a loose or poorly fastened subfloor. Wood subfloors should be securely screwed or nailed down to the joists before new material goes on top. If that step was skipped or rushed, boards can rub against nails, causing noise. If the squeaks are new, it might be that the planks are not fully supported. Uneven subfloors, humps, or dips can create hollow spots that flex when you walk. Floating floors like laminate and vinyl plank need a flat base and the right underlayment. If the floor is squeezed too tight against walls or trim, it can also creak because it cannot move properly. First, note exactly where the squeaks happen and under what conditions. Contact your installer to inspect. In some cases, they can fix the issue from below (in a crawlspace or basement) by adding support or screws. Other times, planks may need to be lifted and the subfloor corrected. The biggest takeaway is that good subfloor prep and following manufacturer guidelines are key. This is why it is important to discuss subfloor work and warranties with your installer before the job starts.
